title: Meta expands Louisiana Hyperion supercluster to 5GW and $50 billion
subtitle: The Richland Parish AI campus keeps growing — from a $10 billion, 2GW build in 2024 to a 5GW, $50-billion-plus site targeting completion around 2032.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Meta said on July 13 that its Hyperion data center campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana will scale to five gigawatts of power capacity and cost more than $50 billion — up from earlier plans for a 2 GW, $10 billion facility. A company spokesperson told CNBC the site will reach 2 GW by 2030 and the full 5 GW by around 2032. Meta framed Hyperion as one of the largest AI infrastructure investments in the world and added more than $1 billion in local infrastructure spending for roads, water, and wastewater.

The expansion signals what post-frontier training runs actually require. Hyperion is not a general-purpose data center — it's packed with GPUs and cooling built for continuous large-model workloads. Five gigawatts is roughly the peak electrical demand of a mid-sized U.S. city, and Meta plans to run all of it from a single AI campus. That is the physical footprint of a company that expects to keep pretraining and inference-serving models substantially larger than what its current fleet supports.

The number tracks the industry's direction of travel. When Meta broke ground in December 2024, Hyperion was priced at $10 billion; by October 2025, its joint venture with Blue Owl Capital pegged the site at $27 billion. The near-doubling in eight months mirrors OpenAI-Oracle's Stargate expansions, xAI's Colossus builds, and the broader hyperscaler capex cycle that Big Tech committed roughly $725 billion to in Q1 alone. Individual sites are now larger than many countries' entire generation capacity.

A note for learners: watch data center scale, not benchmark scores. Model releases get headlines, but the infrastructure that trains and serves them is the leading indicator of what the next generation of AI can do. When a single site jumps from 2 GW to 5 GW in under a year, someone is planning to fill it — with training runs, agents, or both.
